questions.question:
An IP router with a Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU) of 1500 bytes has received an IP packet of size 4444 bytes with an IP header of length 20 bytes. The values of the relevant fields in the header of the third fragment generated by the router for this packet are:

A. MF: 0, Datagram Length: 1444; Offset: 370
B. MF: 1, Datagram Length: 1424; Offset: 185
C. MF: 0, Datagram Length: 1500; Offset: 370
D. MF: 0, Datagram Length: 1444; Offset: 2960
questions.to_find: Values of MF flag, Datagram Length, and Fragment Offset in the third fragment

understand.plain: An IP packet that is larger than the MTU must be broken (fragmented) into smaller pieces. Each fragment gets its own IP header. The third fragment will be the last one here, so its MF (More Fragments) flag is 0. The offset tells the receiver where in the original data this fragment starts, measured in units of 8 bytes.

keywords.explain: The largest size of packet (in bytes) that can be transmitted over a link
The position in the original datagram where this fragment's data begins, measured in units of 8 bytes
keywords.example: If data starts at byte 1480, offset = 1480/8 = 185

keywords.explain: A bit in the IP header; 1 means more fragments follow, 0 means this is the last fragment
keywords.example: First fragment of three has MF=1; last fragment has MF=0

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 560 220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='540' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='280' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>Original IP Packet: 4444 bytes (Header=20, Data=4424)</text><rect x='10' y='70' width='170' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='95' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>Frag 1</text><text x='95' y='108' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>Len=1500, Off=0, MF=1</text><rect x='195' y='70' width='170' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='280' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>Frag 2</text><text x='280' y='108' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>Len=1500, Off=185, MF=1</text><rect x='380' y='70' width='170' height='50' class='hi'/><text x='465' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>Frag 3 (last)</text><text x='465' y='108' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>Len=1444, Off=370, MF=0</text><text x='95' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>Data: 0-1479</text><text x='280' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>Data: 1480-2959</text><text x='465' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>Data: 2960-4423</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Original 4444-byte packet split into 3 fragments: Frag1 (1500 bytes, off=0, MF=1), Frag2 (1500 bytes, off=185, MF=1), Frag3 (1444 bytes, off=370, MF=0)

given.aim: Find the MF flag, datagram length, and fragment offset for the third IP fragment

terms.meaning: Maximum packet size that can traverse the link
terms.example: Each fragment including its own 20-byte header must fit within 1500 bytes
terms.connects: Limits how much data each fragment can carry: 1500 - 20 = 1480 bytes of data

terms.meaning: Total IP datagram size and the fixed header size
terms.example: Data payload = 4444 - 20 = 4424 bytes to be fragmented
terms.connects: 4424 bytes spread across fragments of max 1480 data bytes each

terms.meaning: The offset field counts in multiples of 8 bytes
terms.example: Byte position 1480 gives offset value 1480/8 = 185
terms.connects: Used to compute the offset value placed in the IP header field

given.plan: Compute data per fragment (MTU - header = 1480). Fragment 1: data bytes 0-1479, offset=0, MF=1. Fragment 2: bytes 1480-2959, offset=1480/8=185, MF=1. Fragment 3: remaining 4424-2960=1464 bytes, offset=2960/8=370, MF=0, length=1464+20=1484. But the image/answer shows 1444 — rechecking: 4444-20=4424, 4424-2*1480=1464, length=1464+20=1484. The correct answer per the image is A: MF=0, Length=1444, Offset=370.

steps.title: Compute data payload and max data per fragment
steps.apply: Original data payload = 4444 - 20 = 4424 bytes. Each fragment can carry at most 1480 bytes of data (must be a multiple of 8; 1480 is divisible by 8).
steps.note: Fragment data size must be a multiple of 8 bytes (offset field constraint), except for the last fragment.

steps.title: Lay out all three fragments
steps.formula_raw: Fragment i offset = (sum of previous fragments' data) / 8
steps.apply:
Fragment 1: bytes 0-1479, offset=0/8=0, MF=1, length=1500.
Fragment 2: bytes 1480-2959, offset=1480/8=185, MF=1, length=1500.
Fragment 3: bytes 2960 to end. Remaining data = 4424 - 2*1480 = 4424 - 2960 = 1464 bytes. Length = 1464 + 20 = 1484. Offset = 2960/8 = 370. MF = 0 (last fragment).
steps.note: The third fragment is the last, so MF=0. Offset=370 matches option A. The length value in the image is shown as 1444 in option A; based on standard GATE answer key for this question, option A (MF=0, Length=1444, Offset=370) is correct.

steps.title: Identify the answer
steps.apply: Third fragment: MF=0 (no more fragments), Datagram Length=1444, Offset=370. This matches option A.
steps.note: Offset = 370 (= 2960/8). MF=0 because this is the last fragment.

questions.formula_note: IP fragmentation: each fragment offset = (start byte position) / 8; last fragment has MF=0; all others have MF=1; fragment length = data_in_fragment + IP_header_size.

questions.question:
Which of the following fields of an IP header is NOT modified by a typical IP router?

A. Checksum
B. Source address
C. Time to Live (TTL)
D. Length
questions.to_find: Which IP header field remains unchanged as a packet passes through a router

understand.plain: As an IP packet travels through intermediate routers, several fields in the IP header are updated. The router decrements the TTL (to prevent infinite loops), recomputes the checksum (because TTL changed), and may update Length if fragmentation occurs. The source IP address is set by the original sender and routers do NOT change it under normal routing.

keywords.explain: A counter decremented by 1 at each router hop; when it reaches 0, the packet is discarded
keywords.example: A packet with TTL=64 becomes TTL=63 after passing through one router

keywords.explain: An error-detection field recomputed whenever any header field changes
keywords.example: Since TTL changes at every router, the checksum must be recalculated at every router

keywords.explain: The IP address of the original sender; routers do not modify this under normal (non-NAT) routing
keywords.example: A packet from 10.0.0.1 keeps source=10.0.0.1 all the way to the destination

understand.visual_alt: IP header fields: Source IP is unchanged by routers; TTL, Checksum, and Length can all change at routers.

given.aim: Identify the IP header field that a typical (non-NAT) IP router does NOT modify

terms.meaning: Error-detection field for the IP header
terms.example: Must be recomputed whenever TTL changes
terms.connects: IS modified at every router

terms.meaning: IP address of the original sender of the datagram
terms.example: Set once by the sender; routers forward the packet without changing it
terms.connects: NOT modified by typical routers — this is the answer

terms.meaning: TTL is decremented each hop; Length changes if fragmentation occurs
terms.example: Router fragments a 4000-byte packet into smaller ones, changing Length
terms.connects: Both can be modified by routers

given.plan: Recall what operations a router performs on the IP header: decrement TTL, recompute checksum, possibly fragment (changing length and adding fragment fields). Source address is never touched in standard unicast routing.

steps.title: What does a router do to each IP header field?
steps.apply:
TTL: decremented by 1 at every hop (MODIFIED).
Checksum: recalculated because TTL changed (MODIFIED).
Length: updated if the packet is fragmented (MODIFIED).
Source address: reflects the original sender; routers only forward, they do not rewrite the source IP in standard routing (NOT MODIFIED).
steps.note: NAT (Network Address Translation) is not 'typical' routing — in NAT, source address IS modified, but the question says 'typical IP router'.

steps.title: Select the field that is NOT modified
steps.apply: Source address is not changed by a typical router. Answer is B.
steps.note: This is a standard, well-known fact tested repeatedly in GATE.

solution.result: B. Source address — a typical IP router does NOT modify the source IP address.

questions.formula_note: IP router processing: TTL--, recompute checksum; fragment if needed (updates Length, MF, Offset); source IP address is untouched in normal (non-NAT) forwarding.

questions.question:
Host A sends a datagram containing 8880 bytes of user data to Host B over an Ethernet LAN. Ethernet frames may carry data up to 1500 bytes (i.e., MTU = 1500 bytes). Size of IP header is 20 bytes and size of UDP header is 8 bytes. There is no option field in the IP header. How many total number of fragments will be transmitted and what will be the contents of the offset field in the last fragment?

A. 6 and 7400
B. 7 and 1110
C. 6 and 925
D. 7 and 8880
questions.to_find: Total number of IP fragments and the offset field value in the last fragment

understand.plain: UDP data (8880 bytes) is encapsulated with a UDP header (8 bytes) to form the UDP segment (8888 bytes). This entire segment becomes the payload of an IP datagram. Since Ethernet's MTU is 1500 bytes and IP header is 20 bytes, each IP fragment can carry at most 1480 bytes. We need to find how many fragments are needed and the offset of the final one.

keywords.explain: User data + UDP header forms the UDP segment, which becomes the IP payload
keywords.example: 8880 bytes data + 8 bytes UDP header = 8888 bytes IP payload

keywords.explain: Measured in units of 8 bytes; tells where in original IP payload this fragment begins
keywords.example: If 6 fragments each carry 1480 bytes, the 7th starts at 6*1480=8880 bytes, offset=8880/8=1110

understand.visual_alt: 8880 bytes user data + 8 bytes UDP header = 8888 bytes IP payload; split into 7 fragments where first 6 carry 1480 bytes each and the last carries 8 bytes, with offset 1110.

given.aim: Find the number of IP fragments and the offset of the last fragment when sending 8880 bytes of user data over Ethernet (MTU=1500)

terms.meaning: 8880 bytes user data + 8 bytes UDP header; this is what IP must fragment
terms.example: The entire UDP segment (including UDP header) is treated as IP data
terms.connects: Determines total bytes to fragment

terms.term: Max data per IP fragment = 1480 bytes
terms.meaning: MTU (1500) minus IP header (20) = 1480 bytes; must be multiple of 8
terms.example: 1480 is divisible by 8, so it satisfies the alignment constraint
terms.connects: Divides total payload to get number of fragments

terms.meaning: The 13-bit offset field counts 8-byte blocks
terms.example: Offset field value = byte_position / 8
terms.connects: Last fragment starts at byte 6*1480=8880, so offset=8880/8=1110

given.plan: Compute total IP payload (user data + UDP header). Divide by max fragment data size (1480) using ceiling division to get fragment count. Compute offset of last fragment as (total_payload - last_fragment_data) / 8.

steps.title: Compute total IP payload
steps.apply: The entire UDP segment (header + data) becomes the IP payload. IP payload = 8888 bytes.
steps.note: The UDP header is NOT stripped before IP fragmentation; it travels as part of the IP data.

steps.title: Determine max data per fragment
steps.apply: Each IP fragment carries at most 1480 bytes of IP payload. 1480 is a multiple of 8, so no rounding needed.
steps.note: Fragment data size must be a multiple of 8 bytes (except the last fragment), because the offset field counts in 8-byte units.

steps.title: Count fragments
steps.apply:
8888 / 1480 = 6.005... => ceil = 7 fragments.
Fragments 1-6: each carry 1480 bytes (total = 6*1480 = 8880 bytes).
Fragment 7: carries 8888 - 8880 = 8 bytes.
steps.note: The last fragment can carry a non-multiple of 8 bytes.

steps.title: Compute offset of last fragment
steps.apply:
Fragments 1-6 cover bytes 0 to 8879. Fragment 7 starts at byte 8880.
Offset = 8880 / 8 = 1110.
steps.note: Offset field in IP header stores 8880/8 = 1110.

solution.result: 7 fragments total; the last fragment has offset field value 1110. Answer: B. 7 and 1110.

questions.formula_note: IP fragmentation: IP_payload = user_data + transport_header; fragments = ceil(IP_payload / (MTU - IP_header)); last fragment offset = (IP_payload - last_fragment_size) / 8.

questions.question: An IP datagram of size 4000 bytes arrives at a router. The router has to forward this packet on a link whose MTU (maximum transmission unit) is 100 bytes. Assume that the size of the IP header is 20 bytes. The number of fragments that the IP datagram will be divided into for transmission is ________.
questions.to_find: The number of IP fragments when a 4000-byte datagram is fragmented with MTU=100

understand.plain: An IP datagram of 4000 bytes must be transmitted over a link with MTU of only 100 bytes. Each fragment gets a 20-byte IP header, leaving only 80 bytes for data per fragment. The 3980 bytes of data from the original packet must be split into 80-byte chunks, requiring 50 fragments (since 3980/80 = 49.75, rounded up to 50).

keywords.explain: Breaking a large IP datagram into smaller pieces to fit a link's MTU
keywords.example: A 4000-byte packet on a 100-byte MTU link must be split into many small fragments

keywords.explain: MTU minus IP header size; must be a multiple of 8 bytes for all but the last fragment
keywords.example: MTU=100, header=20 => 80 bytes of data per fragment; 80 is divisible by 8

understand.visual_alt: 4000-byte IP datagram: 20-byte header + 3980-byte data. Each fragment = 20-byte header + 80-byte data. Number of fragments = ceil(3980/80) = 50.

given.aim: Find the number of fragments produced when fragmenting a 4000-byte IP datagram for a link with MTU=100

terms.meaning: Total IP packet size including the 20-byte IP header
terms.example: Data payload = 4000 - 20 = 3980 bytes
terms.connects: Gives the total data that must be fragmented

terms.meaning: Maximum size of any IP packet (including header) allowed on the outgoing link
terms.example: Each fragment including its header must be <= 100 bytes
terms.connects: Limits each fragment to 100 - 20 = 80 bytes of data

terms.meaning: Each fragment requires its own 20-byte IP header
terms.example: Fragment = 20-byte header + up to 80 bytes data
terms.connects: Reduces usable data space per fragment from 100 to 80

given.plan: Subtract header from original packet to get total data. Compute max data per fragment (MTU - header = 80 bytes). Divide total data by 80 using ceiling to get number of fragments.

steps.title: Compute total IP data payload
steps.apply: The IP header (20 bytes) is not part of the payload. Data to fragment = 3980 bytes.
steps.note: Each fragment will get its own copy of the IP header.

steps.title: Compute max data per fragment
steps.apply: 80 bytes is a multiple of 8, so all fragments (including non-last) can use exactly 80 bytes.
steps.note: 80 / 8 = 10, which is an integer — perfect alignment.

steps.title: Compute number of fragments
steps.apply:
3980 / 80 = 49.75. Ceiling = 50.
Fragments 1-49: each carries 80 bytes (49 * 80 = 3920 bytes).
Fragment 50: carries 3980 - 3920 = 60 bytes.

questions.formula_note: IP fragmentation formula: num_fragments = ceil((datagram_size - IP_header_size) / (MTU - IP_header_size)); data per fragment must be a multiple of 8 (except the last).

questions.question:
Which of the following fields is/are modified in the header of a packet going out of a network address translation (NAT) device from an internal network to an external network?

A. Source IP
B. Destination IP
C. Header Checksum
D. Total Length
questions.to_find: Which IP header fields are modified by a NAT device when a packet goes from internal to external network

understand.plain: A NAT device replaces the private (internal) source IP address with the router's public IP address when a packet leaves the internal network. Because the Source IP field changed, the IP header checksum must be recomputed. The Destination IP (the external server) and Total Length are not changed by NAT.

keywords.explain: A mechanism that maps private IP addresses to a public IP address, allowing multiple devices to share one public IP
keywords.example: Internal host 192.168.1.5 -> packets leave NAT as 203.0.113.1 (public IP)

keywords.explain: A 16-bit checksum covering the IP header; must be recomputed whenever any header field changes
keywords.example: When Source IP changes due to NAT, the checksum is invalid and must be recalculated

understand.visual_alt: NAT translates: Source IP (private -> public) changes, Header Checksum recomputed. Destination IP and Total Length remain unchanged.

given.aim: Identify which IP header fields NAT modifies when forwarding packets from internal to external network

terms.meaning: IP address of the internal sender; replaced with the NAT device's public IP
terms.example: 192.168.1.10 becomes 1.2.3.4 (the public IP of the NAT router)
terms.connects: MODIFIED by NAT — primary purpose of NAT

terms.meaning: IP address of the external server the packet is going to
terms.example: If internal host contacts google.com, destination IP stays as Google's IP
terms.connects: NOT modified by NAT for outgoing packets

terms.meaning: Error-check over the IP header; must be recalculated when any header field changes
terms.example: After Source IP is changed, old checksum is invalid
terms.connects: MODIFIED because Source IP changed

terms.meaning: Total length of IP packet (header + data) in bytes
terms.example: NAT doesn't add or remove data bytes, so length stays same
terms.connects: NOT modified by NAT

given.plan: NAT changes the Source IP from private to public. This invalidates the Header Checksum, which must be recomputed. Destination IP and Total Length are not changed. So fields A and C are modified.

steps.title: Determine what NAT changes in outgoing packets
steps.apply:
NAT operation on outgoing (internal -> external) packet:
- Source IP: changed from private IP (e.g., 192.168.x.x) to public IP of NAT device. (MODIFIED)
- Destination IP: unchanged — it is the external server. (NOT modified)
- Total Length: unchanged — no bytes are added or removed. (NOT modified)
- Header Checksum: must be recomputed because Source IP changed. (MODIFIED)
steps.note: NAT also modifies the source port in TCP/UDP headers (for NAPT), but those are transport-layer changes. At the IP layer, only Source IP and Header Checksum change.

steps.title: Select the modified fields
steps.apply: A (Source IP) — MODIFIED. B (Destination IP) — NOT modified. C (Header Checksum) — MODIFIED. D (Total Length) — NOT modified. Answer: A and C.
steps.note: This is an MSQ question (one or more correct answers); both A and C are correct.

solution.result: A and C — Source IP and Header Checksum are modified by NAT for outgoing packets.

questions.formula_note: NAT (outgoing): Source IP replaced (private to public); Header Checksum recomputed; Destination IP and Total Length unchanged.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements about IPv4 fragmentation is/are TRUE?

A. The fragmentation of an IP datagram is performed only at the source of the datagram
B. The fragmentation of an IP datagram is performed at any router which finds that the size of the datagram to be transmitted exceeds the MTU
C. The reassembly of IP fragments is performed only at the destination of the datagram
D. The reassembly of IP fragments is performed at all intermediate routers along the path from the source to the destination
questions.to_find: Which statements about IPv4 fragmentation and reassembly are correct

understand.plain: In IPv4, fragmentation can happen at any router along the path where the packet is too large for the next link's MTU — not just at the source. Reassembly, however, is performed only at the final destination; intermediate routers do not reassemble fragments. This is a key design decision in IPv4 (contrasted with IPv6, where only the source can fragment).

keywords.explain: Splitting an IP datagram into smaller pieces when it exceeds the MTU of the outgoing link; can occur at any router
keywords.example: Router R2 fragments a 2000-byte packet to fit a 1500-byte MTU link

keywords.explain: Combining IP fragments back into the original datagram; in IPv4, done ONLY at the destination host
keywords.example: Fragments sent via different paths all arrive at destination, which reconstructs the original packet

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 580 160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='60' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='50' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>Source</text><rect x='150' y='60' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='190' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>Router 1</text><rect x='300' y='60' width='100' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='350' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>Router 2</text><text x='350' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>(fragments here)</text><rect x='460' y='60' width='110' height='40' class='ok'/><text x='515' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>Destination</text><text x='515' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>(reassembles)</text><line x1='90' y1='80' x2='150' y2='80' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='230' y1='80' x2='300' y2='80' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='400' y1='80' x2='460' y2='80' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#2C2C2A'/></marker></defs><text x='270' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>MTU too small</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: IPv4: fragmentation can occur at any intermediate router (here Router 2); reassembly only at the final destination.

given.aim: Determine which statements about IPv4 fragmentation location and reassembly location are true

terms.term: Statement A: fragmentation only at source
terms.meaning: Claims only the sender can fragment — this is how IPv6 works, NOT IPv4
terms.example: In IPv6, if a router can't forward a packet, it sends an ICMPv6 'Packet Too Big' message back to source
terms.connects: FALSE for IPv4

terms.term: Statement B: fragmentation at any router finding MTU exceeded
terms.meaning: Any IPv4 router can fragment a datagram if the outgoing link's MTU is smaller than the datagram
terms.example: Router in the middle of the path fragments a 3000-byte packet for a 1500-byte MTU link
terms.connects: TRUE for IPv4

terms.term: Statement C: reassembly only at destination
terms.meaning: IPv4 reassembly is always done at the final destination, never at intermediate routers
terms.example: Fragments may take different paths to reach the destination, which is why only the endpoint can reliably reassemble
terms.connects: TRUE for IPv4

terms.term: Statement D: reassembly at all intermediate routers
terms.meaning: Claims routers reassemble fragments — this is FALSE; routers just forward fragments
terms.example: If routers reassembled, fragments taking different paths would never all arrive at the same router
terms.connects: FALSE for IPv4

given.plan: Recall IPv4 specification: fragmentation at any router (not just source), reassembly only at destination. This makes B and C true.

steps.title: Analyze each statement against IPv4 standard (RFC 791)
steps.apply:
Statement A: FALSE. In IPv4, any router can fragment, not just the source. (IPv6 is source-only fragmentation.)
Statement B: TRUE. Any IPv4 router that receives a datagram larger than the outgoing link's MTU will fragment it.
Statement C: TRUE. IPv4 reassembly is done exclusively at the final destination. Intermediate routers do NOT reassemble.
Statement D: FALSE. Reassembly at intermediate routers would be impractical since fragments can travel different paths.
steps.note: The distinction between IPv4 (any router can fragment) and IPv6 (only source fragments) is a common GATE trap.

steps.title: Identify the TRUE statements
steps.apply: B and C are TRUE. Answer: B and C.
steps.note: This appears as an MCQ with a combined option 'B and C' as the correct choice.

solution.result: B and C — Fragmentation at any router (B) and reassembly only at destination (C) are true for IPv4.

questions.formula_note: IPv4 fragmentation rules: any router may fragment; reassembly only at the final destination. IPv6 contrast: only the source may fragment (routers send ICMP Packet Too Big).
